channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: House Adopts Budget to Unlock $70 Billion for Immigration Enforcement
item.description: The measure will allow the G.O.P. to begin working on a filibuster-proof bill to fund ICE and C.B.P., part of their plan to reopen the long-shuttered Department of Homeland Security.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal agents outside a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in Chicago last year.

item.title: Trump Administration Announces Moves to Sharply Curtail Gun Regulations
item.description: The changes are in keeping with the administration’s pledge to cut back on gun restrictions.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.category: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Visitors looked over weapons displayed at the Crossroads of the West Gun Show in Phoenix in January.

item.title: Trump Threatens to Pull Troops From Germany as He Lashes Out at Chancellor
item.description: The comments came after the German chancellor said that Iran has “humiliated” the United States.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: U.S. soldiers on a combat exercise in Bavaria, Germany.

item.title: What the Royal State Dinner Guest List Says About Trump’s America
item.description: There were at least 10 American billionaires, six Fox News hosts, assorted presidential pals, no Democratic politicians and not so many British.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Bumiller
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Elizabeth II, Queen of Great Britain
item.media:description: President Trump and Melania Trump with King Charles III of Britain and Queen Camilla at the White House on Tuesday.

item.title: G.O.P. Congress Struggles to Do the Basics Amid Party Infighting
item.description: House Republicans toiled to pass major measures as lawmakers tried to fund homeland security, extend spy powers and deliver a farm bill.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:description: “Right now we don’t look as functional as we need to look,” Representative Tom Cole, Republican of Oklahoma, acknowledged.

item.title: Hegseth Cites Falsehood to Defend His Firing of Senior Officers
item.description: The defense secretary said at a House hearing that President Barack Obama had fired 197 generals, a figure that the Pentagon previously acknowledged was false.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s actions to fire senior military leaders are without precedent in recent decades and have come with little explanation.

item.title: Jury Delivers Mixed Verdict in Case of Afghan Charged in 2021 Kabul Attack
item.description: Jurors found the man guilty of conspiracy to support the terrorist group that took credit for a horrific bombing, but deadlocked on whether he was responsible for the attack itself.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jim Huylebroek for The New York Times
item.media:description: People being sent away from the scene of a bomb blast outside the airport in Kabul, Afghanistan, on Aug. 26, 2021.

item.title: Surveillance Law Is in Limbo After House Approval
item.description: Republicans put down a right-wing revolt to push a three-year renewal through the House, but the Senate appeared likely to opt for a 45-day punt ahead of a Friday expiration.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage and Michael Gold
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson struggled for more than two hours on Wednesday to round up the votes to bring up the surveillance bill.

item.title: ‘A Lot of Rocket’: Trump Celebrates Artemis II Astronauts at the White House
item.description: President Trump wants Americans going into space to be a part of his legacy, even as he has proposed cutting NASA’s education, research and diversity programs.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:description: President Trump met in the Oval Office on Wednesday with the crew of the Artemis II mission, from left, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, Reid Wiseman and Jeremy Hansen.

item.title: Supreme Court Shakes Up U.S. Political Maps With Voting Rights Act Decision
item.description: Both parties are now scrambling to adjust to a new voting rights landscape.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Administration Sues New Jersey Governor Over ICE Mask Ban
item.description: New Jersey, where Mikie Sherrill is governor, is one of only a few states to try to bar federal immigration agents from wearing masks.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: A masked federal agent guarded a gate at an ICE facility in Newark last year from protesters trying to blockade it.

item.title: Ford Reports Higher Profit Thanks Partly to Tariff Refund
item.description: Ford Motor said it expected the federal government to refund $1.3 billion the company paid in tariffs that the Supreme Court later struck down.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ford Motor vehicles at a dealership in Miami.

item.title: Takeaways From Hegseth’s Testimony on Iran War and His Tenure
item.description: It was Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s first public appearance before Congress since Operation Epic Fury began in late February.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay and Megan Mineiro
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine testified during a congressional hearing on Wednesday.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Anti-Abortion Clinic in Fight Over Donor Records
item.description: A crisis pregnancy group had asked the justices to allow it to sue in federal court over a demand for donor information by New Jersey officials.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s decision was unanimous.

item.title: All Six Conservative Supreme Court Justices Attended Trump’s Dinner With King Charles
item.description: Their appearance seemed at odds with the chief justice’s oft-stated message that the court he leads avoids even the appearance of political splits.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:description: The six conservative Supreme Court justices were invited to the state dinner in the East Room of the White House on Tuesday night.

item.title: James Comey Makes First Court Appearance After Second Indictment Against Him
item.description: The indictment against James B. Comey, the former F.B.I. director, focused on a social media post that the Justice Department deemed a threat to the president.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Journalists gathered outside the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia on Wednesday.

item.title: Pentagon Puts Iran War Cost at $25 Billion as Hegseth Berates Skeptics
item.description: During his first public appearance on Capitol Hill since the war began, the defense secretary lashed out at lawmakers in both parties who have questioned the conflict.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Pete Hegseth, the defense secretary, during a hearing at the Capitol on Wednesday.

item.title: Key Moments in the Life of the Voting Rights Act
item.description: Here’s a look at some key events that led to and followed the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
item.dc:creator: Audra D. S. Burch, Emily Cochrane and Jamie Leventhal

item.title: Springfield, Ohio’s Haitian Workers Face Uncertain Future
item.description: Haitians played a pivotal role in revitalizing the once struggling Ohio city. The end of Temporary Protected Status could be a setback.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Taylor Dorrell for The New York Times
item.media:description: Many Haitians have settled in Springfield, Ohio.

item.title: ‘Excruciating and Agonizing’: A New Reality for Jewish Democrats
item.description: With Israel increasingly unpopular and antisemitism on the rise, Jewish politicians find themselves more and more under attack.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Noah Arbit, a Michigan state representative, said he had made fighting antisemitism a core part of his platform.

item.title: New Details Released About Cole Tomas Allen, Gunman at Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The details, combined with video, seem to suggest that the gunman was not the person who shot an officer in his protective vest.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secret Service agents rushed into the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner after gunshots were fired at the Washington Hilton on Saturday,

item.title: What the Supreme Court’s Voting Rights Ruling Could Mean for the Midterms
item.description: Democrats stand to lose at least one blue-leaning district in Louisiana, but the timing was unclear. Florida has approved a redder map, and Republicans in several other states are weighing new districts.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti, Emily Cochrane and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Marsha Blackburn of Tennessee issued a statement calling on the state legislature to reconvene and pass a new map that eliminates a majority-Black district in Memphis.

item.title: Here’s How the Supreme Court Has Handled Other Humanitarian Protection Cases
item.description: The court has allowed the Trump administration to end protections on a temporary basis in other cases while they are litigated.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: Members of Venezuelan community organizations denounced a Trump administration move to end Temporary Protected Status for Venezuelan migrants in Doral, Fla., last year

item.title: Melania Trump, Queen Camilla and the Look of the Special Relationship
item.description: Parsing a state visit told in photo ops, and style.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.category: Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: In pink: Queen Camilla, King Charles III, President Trump and Melania Trump at the state dinner on Tuesday.

item.title: Anti-Haiti remarks by Trump figure in the Supreme Court arguments.
item.description: The Trump administration argues its effort to end humanitarian protections for Haitians was based on foreign policy and national security, not race.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa and Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: President Trump made accusations during his campaign in 2024.

item.title: How Temporary Is T.P.S.?
item.description: While some from countries with Temporary Protected Status stay for many years, others have been sent home with little fanfare when their countries were deemed safe.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court will weigh the Trump administration’s efforts to end Temporary Protected Status programs for Haiti and Syria.

item.title: Supreme Court Strikes Down Louisiana Map, Another Blow to Voting Rights Act
item.description: The court struck down the voting map as an unconstitutional racial gerrymander in a move that could make it harder for lawmakers to create majority-minority voting districts.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:description: The U.S. Supreme Court on Wednesday.

item.title: United Airlines CEO, Rebuffed by American, Says He’s Skeptical of Other Deals
item.description: The chief executive, Scott Kirby, said that buying a smaller airline might not be worth the effort.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: “Doing a merger takes a lot of calories and a lot of energy,” Scott Kirby, chief executive of United Airlines, said in an interview.

item.title: Which Countries Have T.P.S.?
item.description: The Trump administration has sought to end protections for people from 13 of the 17 countries that had the status.
item.dc:creator: Allison McCann

item.title: These are the lawyers who will be arguing the T.P.S. case.
item.description: The justices will hear from the solicitor general, followed by lawyers for Syrians and Haitians.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:description: Solicitor General D. John Sauer in 2023.

item.title: Officials Distorted Facts to Justify Deporting Haitians, Internal Emails Show
item.description: Emails revealed in a California lawsuit and now submitted to the Supreme Court show that an administration researcher privately disputed claims about Haitians.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:description: Demonstrators protested in January for an extension of Temporary Protected Status for Haitian immigrants.

item.title: Arguments May Shed Light on the Court’s ‘Shadow Docket’
item.description: The Trump administration urged the justices to rely on earlier terse emergency rulings and explain “what to make of this court’s interim orders.”
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court in Washington.

item.title: Justice Amy Coney Barrett Has Personal Ties to Haiti
item.description: Two of Justice Amy Coney Barrett’s seven children were adopted from Haiti.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:description: Justice Amy Coney Barrett has adopted two children from Haiti.

item.title: T.P.S. Clampdown Is Part of Trump’s Broader Immigration Agenda
item.description: It is one of many ways the president has tried to make good on his pledge to deport record numbers of people in his second term.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump speaking in the Oval Office this month.

item.title: What to know about T.P.S. for Haitians and Syrians.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:description: A vigil in Miami in February for Haitians living in the United States under Temporary Protected Status.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow

item.title: Loss of Emirates Further Weakens OPEC’s Influence
item.description: The exit of the United Arab Emirates is the most significant in a series of departures from the oil cartel in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
item.media:description: Oil storage in Big Spring, Texas. OPEC is less powerful than it once was, in no small part because of the rise of U.S. oil production.

item.title: Judge Asks Justice Department: Will You Oppose Trump?
item.description: A federal judge has ordered the Justice Department to explain whether it intends to contest President Trump’s $10 billion lawsuit against the I.R.S. over the disclosure of his tax returns.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:description: President Trump sued the Internal Revenue Service over the leak of his tax returns to news media outlets, arguing that the agency should have done more to prevent the disclosures.

item.title: In First Campaign Ad, Schlossberg Leans on a Well-Known Name: Pelosi
item.description: Jack Schlossberg, the grandson of John F. Kennedy, is among a slate of Democrats seeking to replace Representative Jerrold Nadler of New York.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos

item.title: Supreme Court Grapples With Trump’s Plan to Revoke Deportation Protections
item.description: The case deals with Temporary Protected Status for hundreds of thousands of Haitians and Syrians but could have implications for more than a million from troubled nations.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: A candlelight vigil in Miami in February for Haitians living under Temporary Protected Status, which the Trump administration is trying to end.
